briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond 'ICE-Free Zone' Bylaw Would Restrict City Resources for Immigration Enforcement
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on a proposed ordinance that would update local rules to clearly ban the use of city property, staff, and resources to help carry out federal civil immigration enforcement — meaning ICE (U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement) could not use city facilities as staging areas or detention sites. The proposal would also require staff training, interdepartmental guidelines, and a public communications plan.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote on April 7 and the second reading on April 28 — this could affect your business if you use city facilities.

key_dates.label: Richmond ICE-Free Zone Ordinance — first reading (council vote)
Richmond ICE-Free Zone Ordinance — second reading anticipated
Richmond amended Municipal Code Chapter 2.30 takes effect (if approved)

briefs.title: Council to Consider Proposed Ordinance Limiting City Resources for Immigration Enforcement
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on a proposed ordinance (a local law) that would update Chapter 2.30 of the city's Municipal Code. The proposed change would clarify limits on how city property, staff, and resources can be used to help enforce civil immigration law — meaning federal immigration rules — and would establish communication plans and reporting requirements to support those limits.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect how city spaces and staff interact with federal immigration enforcement near your business.

key_dates.label: Richmond immigration enforcement ordinance — first reading (introduction) at council

briefs.title: Council to Consider Renewing Richmond's $99M Youth Fund for Another 10 Years
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to provide direction on whether to renew the Richmond Fund for Children and Youth (RFCY) — a dedicated funding program that sends 3% of the city's general revenues to youth services each year. Council will choose between renewing it as-is, letting voters decide, or proposing changes (such as adjusting the funding percentage) to go on the November 2026 ballot.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the Council decision — it could affect funding available to youth-serving nonprofits and community organizations.

key_dates.label: Richmond RFCY renewal — Council direction meeting (continued from March 24)
Richmond RFCY community listening sessions — public input opportunity
Richmond RFCY community listening session (second date)
Richmond RFCY renewal — Council study session proposed date
Richmond RFCY renewal — Council resolution deadline to place measure on ballot
Richmond RFCY ballot measure — General Election vote date

briefs.title: Council to vote on $300,000 plant supply contract with Blue Angel International
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on approving a three-year contract with Blue Angel International, LLC to supply trees, shrubs, and groundcover for parks, medians, and other public spaces across the city. The deal would be worth up to $300,000 over three years, with the option to extend for two more years at up to $100,000 per year.
briefs.action_required: Monitor the April 7, 2026 council decision if you supply nursery or landscaping products to public agencies.

key_dates.label: Richmond Council vote — Blue Angel plant supply contract approval

briefs.title: Council to vote on $300,000 bulk landscaping materials contract with Blue Angel International
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on approving a three-year contract worth up to $300,000 with Blue Angel International LLC to supply bulk landscaping materials — such as gravel, compost, sand, and decomposed granite — for city parks, athletic fields, and public rights-of-way. The deal could be extended for two more years at up to $100,000 per year, with the City Manager's sign-off required for any extensions.
briefs.action_required: If you supply landscaping materials, monitor future City of Richmond procurement postings for similar opportunities.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Natural Resources, Transportation & Logistics

briefs.title: Council to vote on $1.25M contract for city-wide road striping and street sign services
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on approving a contract with Chrisp Company to provide on-call road striping, pavement markings, and street sign installation and replacement services across the city. The proposed contract would be worth up to $750,000 over three years, with the option to extend for two more years at up to $250,000 per year, for a total of up to $1,250,000.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on April 7, 2026 — this affects road contractors and sign service providers.

key_dates.label: Richmond Council vote — Chrisp Company road striping contract approval

briefs.title: Council to Consider Ordering Engineer's Report for Hilltop Landscape Maintenance District 2026–2027
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on starting the annual process to collect landscape maintenance fees from property owners in the Hilltop area. The first step is ordering an Engineer's Report — a document that outlines what maintenance work is planned, what it will cost, and how much each property owner will be charged for the 2026–2027 fiscal year.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing notice — property owners in the Hilltop district will have a chance to comment on proposed fees.

key_dates.label: Richmond Council vote — order Hilltop Landscape Maintenance District Engineer's Report

briefs.title: Council to Consider Ordering Engineer's Report for Marina Bay Landscape and Lighting District
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on starting the annual process to collect special fees — called benefit assessments — from property owners in the Marina Bay Landscape and Lighting Maintenance District. The first step is ordering an Engineer's Report for the 2026–2027 fiscal year, which will outline maintenance costs and how fees are divided among properties in the district.
briefs.action_required: If you own property in Marina Bay, watch for the upcoming public hearing where you can comment on proposed assessment amounts.

key_dates.label: Marina Bay Landscape and Lighting District — Engineer's Report ordered (council vote)

briefs.title: Council to vote on $785,350 construction contract for Marina Bay environmental cleanup cap
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on approving a construction agreement with O.C. Jones Inc. to install a temporary environmental cap — a protective cover placed over contaminated soil to stop pollutants from spreading — at Area FM in Marina Bay. The total project cost could reach up to $942,050 (including a 20% backup budget for unexpected costs), with an additional $450,000 to be drawn from the Marina Enterprise Fund to cover all related expenses.
briefs.action_required: Monitor the April 7, 2026 council meeting outcome if your business operates near Marina Bay.

key_dates.label: Richmond Council vote — Area FM Temporary Cap construction agreement approval

briefs.title: Council to vote on Public Works contracts and infrastructure spending in Richmond
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on several Public Works items at its April 7, 2026 meeting. These include a proposed construction contract worth up to $785,350 with O.C. Jones & Sons for a marina cap project, a road-marking and traffic-signage contract up to $750,000 with Chrisp Company, and two separate landscaping supply contracts with Blue Angel International totalling up to $600,000 over three years — all subject to council approval.
briefs.action_required: Check if your business supplies or bids on city contracts — these awards may affect upcoming procurement opportunities.

key_dates.label: Richmond City Council public works vote — meeting date

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Transportation & Logistics, Professional Services

briefs.title: Council to vote on $300,000 contract for police data reporting and video redaction software
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on approving a sole-source contract (meaning only one vendor is being considered, without a competitive bid) with Veritone, Inc. worth up to $300,000 over three years. The contract would cover software that helps the police department meet two state legal requirements: submitting stop data under California's Racial and Identity Profiling Act, and blurring sensitive details in body camera and audio footage before releasing records to the public.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this contract affects how police handle public records requests.

key_dates.label: Veritone contract proposed start date — Richmond Police Department
Veritone contract proposed end date — Richmond Police Department

briefs.title: Council to vote on $1.35M annual police service contracts with county and state
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on authorizing the City Manager to sign recurring contracts totalling $1,350,000 per year with Contra Costa County and the State of California for essential police services. These contracts cover things like forensic lab analysis, fingerprinting, officer training, jail access, and radio systems — services the Richmond Police Department relies on daily to operate.

briefs.title: Council to Receive Richmond Police Department Monthly Crime Report for January 2026
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to receive the Police Department's monthly crime report for January 2026. Chief Tim Simmons will present data on violent crimes, property crimes, calls for service, parking enforcement, and notable incidents from that month. There is no financial impact tied to receiving this report.

briefs.title: Council to vote on $46,232 ballistic helmet purchase for Richmond SWAT Team
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on approving the purchase of Ops-Core FAST ballistic helmets from Adamson Police Products for the Richmond Police Department's SWAT Team. The total cost would be up to $46,232.20, mostly covered by a federal Justice Assistance Grant, with a small remainder drawn from the existing police budget.

briefs.title: Council to vote on police equipment, law enforcement service contracts, and RIPA compliance software
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on several Police Department items at its April 7, 2026 meeting. These include approving up to $46,232 for SWAT ballistic helmets, renewing annual law enforcement service contracts with Contra Costa County and the State of California totalling $1.35 million per year for fiscal years 2027–2030, and approving a sole-source contract (meaning only one supplier was considered) with Veritone, Inc. for up to $300,000 to support racial profiling reporting under the RIPA Act and audio/video redaction required by public records law.
briefs.action_required: Review the Veritone contract if your business handles public records or law enforcement data — it may affect compliance expectations.

key_dates.label: Veritone RIPA compliance contract start date — Richmond Police Department
Veritone RIPA compliance contract end date — Richmond Police Department
Contra Costa/California law enforcement service contracts term begins — Richmond PD

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Proclaiming April 14, 2026 as Nepali New Year in Richmond
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council will consider issuing a proclamation recognizing April 14, 2026 as Nepali New Year (Naya Barsha 2083 Bikram Sambat). This is a ceremonial gesture — meaning it carries no legal weight or financial cost — to honour the cultural contributions of Richmond's Nepalese community.

briefs.title: Council to Consider Reappointing Bryan M Harris to Richmond Commission on Aging
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council will consider reappointing Bryan M Harris to the Commission on Aging, a city advisory body focused on issues affecting older adults. If approved, his new term would run until May 19, 2030. Harris is a retired nurse and long-time Richmond resident who has already served over three years on the commission.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Appointing Kathleen Tarr to Richmond's Urban Forest Advisory Committee
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on appointing Kathleen Tarr to a vacant seat on the Urban Forest Advisory Committee, with a term running until November 17, 2029. If approved, Ms. Tarr — who is affiliated with Harvard Alumni for Climate and the Environment — would help support the city's tree planting, urban canopy, and climate resilience goals.

briefs.title: Council to vote on reappointing Thomas Kaun to Richmond Library Commission
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council will consider reappointing Thomas Kaun to the Library Commission at its April 7, 2026 meeting. If approved, his new term would run until July 1, 2029. Kaun has served on the commission for three years and focuses on improving library services for seniors and people with disabilities.

briefs.title: Council to vote on: Reappointment of Thomas Kaun to Richmond Library Commission
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on reappointing Thomas Kaun to the Library Commission, with a term set to expire on July 1, 2029. This is a routine consent calendar item — meaning it is grouped with other non-controversial items and approved together in a single vote unless a council member or member of the public requests it be pulled for separate discussion.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision if you have an interest in Richmond Library Commission membership.

key_dates.label: Richmond Library Commission — Kaun reappointment term expires

briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to vote on updated fire and wildfire safety codes
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on adopting the 2025 California Fire Code and a brand-new California Wildland-Urban Interface Code — both updated state safety rules — along with local add-ons tailored to Richmond's specific risks. The local changes would tighten rules around fire sprinklers, emergency access roads, above-ground fuel tanks, fireworks liability, and building standards in high wildfire-risk areas.
briefs.action_required: Check if your building or business operations fall under the updated sprinkler, access road, or wildfire zone rules.

key_dates.label: Knox key box system upgrade compliance deadline — Richmond Fire Code

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Transportation & Logistics

briefs.title: Council to vote on adopting 2025 California Fire Code with local amendments
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on a proposed ordinance (second reading) that would update the city's fire prevention rules by adopting the 2025 California Fire Code and the 2025 California Urban-Wildland Interface Code — both with local amendments. This would replace the current fire code and could affect how businesses must meet fire safety and building standards going forward.
briefs.action_required: Review the 2025 California Fire Code amendments to check if your business faces new fire safety requirements.

briefs.title: Council to Consider Monthly Finance Reports for Richmond, California — February 2026
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council (California) is set to receive a package of routine monthly financial reports for February 2026, covering the city's investments and cash balances, department overtime spending, property transfer tax revenue, and pension and retiree benefit fund performance. No spending decisions or new rules are attached — this is an informational review only, with no financial impact on the city budget.

briefs.title: Council to vote on $59,230 contract to study and update Richmond's development and service fees
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on hiring Willdan Financial Services to conduct two fee studies: one examining what new developments should pay toward road improvements (a Traffic Impact Fee Study), and one reviewing whether city service fees — like permits and inspections — reflect their true costs (a Comprehensive User Fee Study). The contract would run from April 2026 to June 2028 and cost up to $59,230. If approved, the findings could lead to updated fees that affect developers and businesses that pay for city services.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — updated fees could affect your permit and development costs.

key_dates.label: Willdan Financial Services contract start date — Richmond fee studies begin
Richmond Traffic Impact and User Fee Study contract end date

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Professional Services, Finance & Insurance

briefs.title: Council to vote on hiring firm to study traffic impact fees and user fees in Richmond
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on approving a contract worth up to $59,230 with Willdan Financial Services to conduct a Traffic Impact Fee Study and a Comprehensive User Fee Study — reviews that examine what fees the city charges for services and development. Separately, Council will consider direction on renewing the Richmond Fund for Children and Youth (Article 15), which currently sets aside 3% of the city's General Fund for youth programs and expires after FY 2027–28.
briefs.action_required: Watch for council's decision on fee studies — results could lead to new or higher fees affecting your business.

briefs.title: Council to vote on weed control contract for Port of Richmond marine terminal
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on approving a contract with T&R Riparian Restore to handle weed removal at the Point Potrero Marine Terminal. The proposed deal would cover one year at up to $69,785, with two optional one-year extensions — bringing the total possible value to $209,355.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 7, 2026 council decision if you operate near or do business with the Port of Richmond.

key_dates.label: Richmond Council vote on T&R Riparian Restore weed abatement contract

briefs.industries: Transportation & Logistics, Natural Resources, Construction & Development

briefs.title: Council to Receive Update: Richmond Green-Blue New Deal Progress on Jobs and Clean Energy
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to receive a quarterly status update on the Green-Blue New Deal (GBND) — a city plan aimed at creating at least 1,000 new jobs in clean energy, port upgrades, and environmental work. The update covers progress from October to December 2025 across five priority areas, including offshore wind planning, building electrification training, brownfield cleanup jobs, downtown revitalization, and the city's shift to zero-emission vehicles.
briefs.action_required: Check if your business qualifies for the Small Business Beautification Pilot Program — applications are now open.

key_dates.label: Richmond Small Business Beautification Pilot Program — application window open

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Transportation & Logistics, Natural Resources

briefs.title: Council to vote on $1.08M contract with Dynamo Construction for Berth 7 Dock Repair
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on approving a contract with Dynamo Construction to repair a damaged structural beam and related dock components at Berth 7 of the Port of Richmond. The proposed contract would not exceed $1,080,650 — covering a base bid of $830,650 plus a $250,000 contingency for unexpected issues — with work to be completed by December 31, 2027.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on April 7, 2026 — this affects port infrastructure and construction activity.

key_dates.label: Berth 7 Dock Repair contract completion deadline — Port of Richmond

briefs.title: Council to vote on Economic Development contracts including $1M Pier 7 repair
briefs.summary: Richmond Council is set to vote on several economic development contracts at its April 7, 2026 meeting. Up for approval is a contract worth up to $1,080,650 with Dynamo Construction to repair Pier 7, running until December 2027, plus a weed-removal services contract with T&R Riparian Restore worth up to $209,355 over three years. Council will also receive a quarterly progress update on the Richmond Green-Blue New Deal projects.
briefs.action_required: Review the Pier 7 and Green-Blue New Deal items if your business operates near the waterfront or in construction.

briefs.title: Council to Consider Accepting $246,601 in Workforce Development Grants for Richmond Programs
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on accepting $246,601 in grant funding from four sources — Construction Trades Workforce Initiative, Chevron, Pinole Youth Foundation, and California's Employment Development Department — to support job training, a youth trades fair, and services for laid-off workers. No city tax dollars are involved; all spending would be covered by the grants.
briefs.action_required: If you work in construction trades or employ young workers, watch for the Youth Trade Fair in late April 2026.

key_dates.label: Richmond Youth Trade Fair — Construction Trades Workforce Initiative event

briefs.title: Council to vote on accepting $4,500 Kaiser Permanente sponsorship for Park Prescription Day
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on accepting a $4,500 sponsorship from Kaiser Permanente to fund Park Prescription Day, a free community health event planned for April 25, 2026 at Nicholl Park. The funds would cover activities like fitness classes, healthcare access, and family games — at no cost to the city's general budget.
briefs.action_required: If your business wants to table or host activities at the event, contact Recreation at 510-620-6793.

key_dates.label: Park Prescription Day free community event at Nicholl Park

briefs.title: Council to vote on accepting $246,601 in workforce development and training grants
briefs.summary: Richmond Council is set to vote on accepting over $246,000 in workforce development grants from multiple funders — including Chevron, the Pinole Youth Foundation, and the California Employment Development Department — to support job training programs, youth employment fairs, and rapid-response layoff services. Council will also consider accepting a $4,500 Kaiser Permanente sponsorship to support a Park Prescription Day community health event.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision if your business participates in or benefits from local workforce training programs.

briefs.title: Council to Consider Updating City-Wide Records Retention and Destruction Schedule
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on April 21, 2026, on updates to the city's Records Retention and Destruction Schedule — the official rules that say how long the city must keep different types of documents before they can be destroyed. The Police Department's records are not included and will be reviewed separately at a later date.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 21, 2026 council decision — this is unlikely to directly affect most businesses.

key_dates.label: Richmond Records Retention and Destruction Schedule update — council vote

briefs.title: Council to Consider Routine Consent Calendar Items — Richmond, April 7 2026
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on a package of routine, non-controversial items grouped under the Consent Calendar at its April 7, 2026 meeting. A Consent Calendar is a common council tool where multiple low-controversy items are approved together in a single vote, with no individual debate — none of these items have been approved yet.
briefs.action_required: Check the full Richmond Council agenda for April 7, 2026 to see if any items affect your business.

key_dates.label: Richmond City Council Consent Calendar vote — April 7 2026 meeting

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Richmond Open Forum for Public Comment — April 7, 2026
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to hold an open public comment session on April 7, 2026, where residents and business owners can speak for up to 2 minutes on items still on the Consent Calendar (a group of routine agenda items voted on together) or on topics not listed on the agenda. Back-and-forth discussion with Council members is not permitted during this session.
briefs.action_required: Attend the April 7 meeting to share your comments — prepare a clear 2-minute statement in advance.

key_dates.label: Richmond Council open public comment forum — attendance deadline

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Richmond Fund for Children & Youth 10-Year Renewal Options
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to consider whether to renew the Richmond Fund for Children and Youth (a programme that sets aside a portion of city general fund revenue for youth services) for another 10-year term running to 2038. Council will choose between renewing it directly by majority vote, sending the question to voters on the November 2026 ballot, or proposing amendments — such as changing the funding level from 3% to 2% or 1% of general fund revenue — before putting it to a public vote.
briefs.action_required: Watch the November 2026 ballot and council vote — the outcome affects city funding priorities and local tax allocation.

briefs.title: Council to consider renewing Richmond's Children & Youth Fund for 10 more years
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on whether to extend the Richmond Fund for Children and Youth — a program that sets aside a portion of the city's general fund revenue for youth services — for another 10-year term (FY 2029–2038). Council will consider three options: renewing it directly by majority vote, sending it to voters without changes, or proposing amendments (such as adjusting the funding percentage from 3% to 2% or 1%) and placing those changes on the November 2026 ballot for public approval.

briefs.title: Council to consider renewing Richmond Fund for Children and Youth — 10-year extension up for approval
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to provide direction on whether to renew the Richmond Fund for Children and Youth (Article 15 of the City Charter) for another 10-year term running from 2029 to 2038. Council will consider three options: renewing the fund by a majority vote (no public vote needed), letting voters decide on the November 2026 ballot without changes, or sending an amended version to voters — which could include adjusting the fund's share of the General Fund from the current 3% down to 2% or 1%.
briefs.action_required: Watch for Council's direction — changes to this fund could affect youth-serving businesses and grant recipients.

briefs.title: Council to vote on Richmond Housing Authority's 2026 Annual Plan and Five-Year Plan
briefs.summary: The Richmond Housing Authority (RHA) Board of Commissioners is set to vote on approving its Fiscal Year 2026 Annual Plan and FY 2026–2030 Five-Year Plan. These documents are required by the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) — the federal agency that funds public housing — and must be submitted to keep the RHA eligible for capital funding used to maintain and improve public housing properties.

briefs.title: Council to consider renewing Richmond's children and youth fund for 10 more years
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on whether to renew the Richmond Fund for Children and Youth — a program that sets aside a portion of the city's general fund revenue to support youth programs and services — for another 10-year term running from 2029 to 2038. Council will consider three options: renew it directly by majority vote (no public vote needed), send it to voters on the November 2026 ballot without changes, or propose amendments and send those to voters.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — it determines whether this fund continues, changes, or goes to a public vote.

key_dates.label: Richmond Fund for Children and Youth renewal — council reauthorization deadline
Richmond Fund for Children and Youth renewal — November general election ballot deadline

briefs.title: Council to consider renewing Richmond's $99M youth fund for another 10 years
briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to decide whether to renew the Richmond Fund for Children and Youth — a program that sets aside 3% of the city's general fund revenue each year for youth programs and services. Council will consider three options: renew it themselves by majority vote (no public vote needed), send it to voters in November 2026 unchanged, or propose amendments and send those to voters. If Council takes no action by June 13, 2026, the measure must go on the November 2026 ballot.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the June 13 council decision — it determines whether voters get a say on this fund's renewal.
